data.title: Diesel lorries to be banned in eco drive
data.description:
Labour has unveiled plans to introduce a petrol and diesel lorry ban as part of their net-zero drive.

Ministers have announced an end to the sale of new fossil fuel-powered trucks in a move that opens up a new battle with the Tories and Reform.

They have ruled out allowing the continued use of low-carbon or synthetic fuels, meaning that from 2040 all new heavy goods vehicles (HGVs) will have to be electric.

Critics warned that forcing haulage companies to adopt expensive battery or hydrogen-powered vehicles would push up the cost of business.

The extra expense would then be passed onto consumers in the form of higher prices for goods like food and clothes, as well as parcel deliveries.

Richard Holden, the Tory shadow transport secretary, said: “Labour are sleepwalking into a cost shock for the entire economy.

“Forcing haulage firms to scrap perfectly good lorries while they can still run for years and replace them with other vehicles before either the technology or infrastructure is ready will simply drive up costs, as well as being environmentally disastrous.

“Those costs will be passed straight through supply chains into higher prices in shops, higher construction costs, and more pressure on inflation. Every supermarket shelf, building site, and home delivery will feel higher prices.

“Rather than letting innovation and the market cut emissions sensibly, Labour are reaching straight for quotas, caps and bans. Central planning with a delivery charge attached is delivering another blow to the British economy.”

The move risks exposing Labour to further attacks from the Tories and Reform, who have both pledged to roll back costly net zero policies.

Both parties have said they would scrap the 2030 petrol car ban and cut subsidies, which would cost taxpayers billions a year.

The 2040 date for phasing out petrol trucks was first proposed by the Tories in November 2021 at the height of Boris Johnson’s net zero ambitions.

In a consultation document published last week, the Department for Transport set out three options for enforcing the new petrol lorry ban.

The first would see manufacturers set annual quotas for the number of electric HGVs they must sell, similar to how the petrol car ban will work.

Another option laid out by ministers is to follow the EU’s approach and set haulage companies ever decreasing carbon emissions caps.

Finally, delivery firms could be required to ensure that an ever increasing proportion of their fleet was electric, eventually reaching 100 per cent.

Chris Ashley, from the Road Haulage Association (RHA), said: “We’re clear that the decarbonisation transition hinges on two key issues being addressed – infrastructure in place to power zero emission HGVs, and vehicle affordability.

“The pathway to decarbonise lorries and coaches in the UK must be realistic and viable – we’ll look to see how the consultation achieves that.”

In a survey run by the RHA last year, seven in 10 haulage firms said they had no plans to add any zero-emission vehicles to their fleets. The main barriers cited were the extra costs involved in buying electric-powered lorries and concerns over their lack of range.

Figures from February last year show that there were just 500 electric HGVs registered in the UK out of a total fleet of 535,000.

Extra subsidies needed

Separately, the Green Finance Institute has estimated £100bn worth of additional finance and subsidies would be needed to support electric lorry take-up.

More than 80 per cent of goods are transported around Britain by road, meaning an increase in cost for hauliers could have a significant impact on prices.

The logistics sector also employs almost 2.7 million workers, with its more than 200,000 businesses adding £185bn to the UK economy every year.

For smaller trucks up to 26 tonnes, the sale of new petrol and diesel-powered models will be banned from as early as 2035 under the plans.

Keir Mather, the minister for transport decarbonisation, said the Government was aware it would be “challenging” to decarbonise the sector. But he insisted there was a need to act because lorries “have a high environmental impact” and emit 16 per cent of all emissions from domestic transport.

“That is why we are firmly committed to accelerating the transition to clean technologies and to setting a path to phase out the sale of…all new non-zero emission heavy goods vehicles by 2040,” he wrote.

He claimed technological advances would lead to “ever better, cheaper and more capable vehicles” that would ultimately bring down costs for haulage firms.

data.title: Netflix sweetens £62bn offer for Warner Bros in Hollywood bid battle
data.description:
Netflix has sweetened its $83bn (£62bn) takeover bid for Warner Bros after tabling a new cash-only offer to fend off rival Hollywood bidder Paramount.

The streaming giant on Tuesday said it would offer $27.75 per share in cash. Shareholders had previously been offered $23.25 in cash and $4.50 of Netflix shares, a deal Paramount had labelled too “complex”.

Netflix and Paramount, run by David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry Ellison, have been going head-to-head for control of Warner Bros for months in an increasingly bitter battle between the Hollywood giants.

Netflix said the updated bid, which was unanimously approved by the Warner Bros board, would provide greater certainty for shareholders and accelerate the takeover. The company added that it expects investors to vote on the deal by April.

The US giant is trying to shore up support from shareholders amid growing pressure from Paramount, which is attempting to gatecrash the deal with its own $108bn takeover bid.

Paramount has previously criticised Netflix’s bid as overly complex and warned that the value to shareholders was dependent on fluctuations in the streaming giant’s share price.

The studio has also criticised a lack of clarity over how Warner Bros values its traditional cable business, which owns networks such as CNN.

Under the terms of the Netflix deal, Warner Bros will spin off the cable business into a separate company called Discovery Global prior to the deal taking place. By contrast, Paramount has tabled a bid for the entire group.

In a stock market filing on Tuesday, Warner Bros said its advisers had valued Discovery Global at a broad range between 72¢ and $1.65 per share. It said shareholders would receive this additional value once the spin-off had been completed.

Warner Bros also said Discovery Global’s debt would be reduced by $260m thanks to improved cash flow. As a result, the division is expected to have net debt of $17bn, falling to $16.1bn by the end of the year.

Netflix’s all-cash bid will increase pressure on Paramount to improve its own approach. The company has previously sweetened its bid by saying Larry Ellison would provide a personal guarantee for $40bn in equity, but has not increased the value of the offer.

Paramount argues that a merger between Netflix and Warner Bros would pose significant competition risks as it would lead to the merger of two major streaming services – Netflix and HBO Max.

It has also warned of a risk to cinemas, arguing that Netflix could reduce the number of films released theatrically.

Mr Ellison last week met Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy as the company ramps up its lobbying efforts across Europe. Senior executives have also held meetings with Ofcom and the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA).

Netflix has played down competition concerns and insisted it is committed to releasing films in cinemas. Nevertheless, it has acknowledged that regulatory reviews could take up to 18 months.

David Zaslav, the president and chief executive of Warner Bros Discovery, said: “Today’s revised merger agreement brings us even closer to combining two of the greatest storytelling companies in the world, and with it, even more people enjoying the entertainment they love to watch the most.

“By coming together with Netflix, we will combine the stories Warner Bros has told that have captured the world’s attention for more than a century and ensure audiences continue to enjoy them for generations to come.”

data.title: Goldman Sachs traders eye push into gambling on prediction markets
data.description:
Goldman Sachs traders are eyeing a push into the world of gambling on prediction markets.

David Solomon, the bank’s chief executive, said he had met with the bosses of major prediction markets in recent weeks and wanted to understand the opportunities on offer.

Traders are eyeing a push into the space after an explosion in their popularity over the last year.

Prediction markets allow individuals to bet on the outcomes of events as diverse as whether the US would bomb Iran to who would win the Golden Globes. The two largest markets in the US are Kalshi and Polymarket, which accepts bets in cryptocurrency.

Mr Solomon said: “I personally met with the two big prediction companies and their leadership in the last two weeks and spent a couple of hours with each to learn more about that. 

“We have a team of people here that are spending time with them and are looking at it.”

Polymarket was banned from operating in the US under the Biden administration, however, Donald Trump has lifted restrictions as part of a broader embrace of the crypto market.

The platform rose to prominence after users correctly predicted Mr Trump’s resounding win in the 2024 election, despite pollsters predicting a much tighter race.

Kalshi, a similar platform, was created by Tarek Mansour, who came up with the concept while working as an equity derivatives intern at Goldman Sachs in 2016.

At the time, institutions were scrambling to guard against the fallout from Brexit, giving Mr Mansour the idea of an exchange to trade events themselves rather than financial assets.

While these markets effectively amount to gambling on the outcome of world events, they are considered to be financial derivative products in the US and are regulated in the same way as futures or options contracts that Goldman and other banks already trade.

‘Opportunity’ to scale

Mr Solomon’s comments came as Goldman Sachs retook its crown as the best bank on Wall Street for stock trading after a record $4.3bn (£3.2bn) haul for the final three months of last year.

The profit from trading was almost $700m higher than what analysts had expected. It ended Morgan Stanley’s three-month spell as the top stock trading bank on Wall Street, a spot that Goldman had previously held since the end of 2022.

On a busy day for results in Wall Street, Goldman and its rival Morgan Stanley both reported a rise in profits in the fourth quarter.

Mr Solomon added that corporate bosses are trying to use the next three years of the Trump administration to pursue major deals.

He said: “I think CEOs and boards are looking and saying, OK, we’ve got a window here of a handful of years where the opportunity to consider big, strategic, transformative things is certainly possible.

“CEOs definitely believe that the art of the deal and scaled consolidation is possible now.”

BlackRock, the world’s biggest asset manager, also broke records on Thursday as it reported assets under management of over $14tn for the first time.

It pulled in $342bn of new client money, beating Wall Street estimates. Of this, $12.7bn came from private markets.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: How the 50/30/20 budgeting hack can unlock your finances
data.description:
Budgeting is the key to running your finances smoothly. Whether you have a high or low income – or somewhere in between – a budget is important to maintain the levels of spending and saving you can afford.

Getting a handle on your income and outgoings can help reset your finances and spending patterns by assessing where your money goes every month.

A budget can also be a real help in achieving specific financial goals you might have. This could be saving for a summer holiday or a new car, or raising a deposit for a house, a wedding, or even paying off debts.

There are many different budgeting methods to choose from.

The 50/30/20 rule is a straightforward budgeting method that can help you to manage your money simply and effectively so you live within your means and reach your goals – whether that’s to spend less to pay off debts or to save more for your future.

How the 50/30/20 rule works

This method of budgeting suggests dividing your monthly income into three spending categories: 50pc for “needs”, 30pc for “wants” and 20pc for “financial goals”.

The 50pc towards needs will be the portion that covers all the regular essential outgoings such as your mortgage or rent, and household bills such as gas and electricity, water, mobile phone, house insurance, as well as the cost of running a car if you have one.

You would then set aside 30pc for wants, which pays for things like hobbies and sports, socialising, shopping and subscriptions for TV, music streaming or newspapers and magazines.

The remaining 20pc can be used for financial goals, such as paying off debt, contributing to savings, making longer-term investments or saving into a pension.

The 50/30/20 rule can work, in theory, whatever your income level.

Someone earning £34,000 (around the UK national average) would take home £28,000 a year or £2,333 a month, according to calculations by Hargreaves Lansdown, an investment platform.

According to the 50/30/20 rule, this would give you £1,166 a month to spend on needs, £700 on wants, and £467 on savings. With a 5pc pension contribution – which is £142 a month – that would leave you £325 to put aside in the savings pot.

A higher earner on a salary of £60,000 would take home £46,357 a year, or £3,780 a month.

It would give you £1,890 a month to spend on needs, £1,134 on wants and £756 on savings. A 5pc pension contribution – which is £250 a month – means you’re left with £506 to save or repay debts.

If you had a credit card debt of £5,000, you would be able to clear it between 13 and 16 months if you used the budgeting rule.

Equally, this amount could go towards your £20,000 annual tax-free Isa allowance.

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “How you use the savings money will depend on your circumstances.

“Some will need to pay down expensive short-term debts, while others will want to build emergency savings, their pension or investments – often people will want to do all of these things, so will split the cash between the different goals.”

Bending the budgeting rules

One big problem with this method is that it may not be possible to get all your essential bills into the 50pc bracket.

Ms Coles said: “The proportions won’t work for everyone, largely because the cost of putting a roof over your head is so high in the UK.”

Ms Coles calculated that if you took a middle-income household that earns £32,651 after tax – or £2,721 a month – the cost of their essentials is £1,755 a month, which is 65pc of their income.

If you then put 30pc aside for wants, you’re left with next to nothing to save for the future.

It means you need to flex your “wants” to fit the cash available, and free up enough money for the future, which might work out as 65pc needs, 15pc wants and 20pc savings.

To cut the cost of your “wants”, you’ll need to come up with some sacrifices.

Cutting out one of the restaurant meals you have each month can mean money is redirected to the needs pot, for example. You might also need to ruthlessly cut shopping trips and subscriptions to achieve your goals.

If that doesn’t work for you, you may find that other breakdowns are more achievable – and these could change during the year as life dictates different spending patterns.

Ms Coles added: “Simple rules of thumb like the 50/30/20 can be useful for keeping you roughly on track. However, it’s not a good idea to try to follow them without calculating whether they’ll work for you, or they could lead you astray.”

Timeless budgeting tips

Sticking to a budget can be tricky, and if you’ve already tried a method that didn’t work for you it can be easy to give up.

Budgeting isn’t a one-size-fits-all situation. If you don’t think you can stick to such a strict regime such as the 50/30/20 rule, some of these key budgeting tips can help you stay in control of your money.

1. Understand where your money goes

You might roughly know what you spend your money on each month, but it will help to know your exact outgoings – and what they’re spent on.

If you don’t review your spending often, one way to do this is to go through your bank statements either online or on a banking app and make a list of the bills you pay as well as amounts for online subscriptions, eating out, travel, supermarket shopping, clothes, sports and other treats.

The list might be long, but you can then see where your earnings go – and where any savings can be made.

Many banking apps will automatically categorise your spending so you can easily see whether a large proportion of your money goes on eating out.

If yours doesn’t, then apps such as Moneyhub, Snoop and Emma can do it for you.

2. Know your goals

Each of us has different goals and things we want to save up for – whether it’s a holiday, home improvements or a wedding – or debts to pay off.

Keeping these in mind when thinking about your spending and saving means you have a better chance of reaching them.

3. ‘No spend’ weekends

When an unexpected expense comes your way, such as a car repair or faulty boiler, you may feel the pinch.

A no-spend weekend can be a great way to offset any overspending you’ve done. It involves going 48 hours without tapping any card or buying online. It’s not necessarily about giving up things you enjoy doing, but rather an opportunity to get creative with ways to have fun for free.

This can also be a way of preparing for an expense, boosting your savings.

For an even bigger challenge, you could join the people making “no spend” months popular.

4. Be open about your money mission

Tell friends and family if you’re on a strict money diet so they can support you.

This tactic – also known as “loud budgeting” – can hopefully mean you won’t be embarrassed to opt out of expensive dinners out and any other pricey get-togethers, as you’ll have already made it clear that you’re keeping your spending to a minimum.

5. Seek help if you need it

Some restructuring of your borrowing might be enough to help you regain control.

But if this and some smart budgeting aren’t enough to resolve your money problems, then don’t shy away from seeking help.

Debt advice is best sought from a debt charity, which will advise you, and act on your behalf, with nothing to pay for their services.

Help from a debt charity will have an immediate benefit as they can often negotiate freezing interest payments on some debts to give you some breathing space while you work out a plan for the road ahead.

Beware of debt help or loan consolidation companies that market their services heavily. They will often charge for advice and these fees will only serve to add to your existing debts.

Try the National Debtline (0808 808 4000), StepChange Debt Charity (0800 138 1111), or the Debt Advice Foundation (0800 043 40 50).

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: ‘Un-pensioning’ could set a very dangerous precedent indeed
data.description:
Join Michael in the comment section below from 11am.

At least 150 teachers have been banned from schools over the last 10 years for child sexual abuse.

The true figure could be considerably higher as Freedom of Information Act requests show that since 2015 a further 256 were struck off for non-defined sexual misconduct and another 245 for “breach of boundaries/trust”.

Yet all these nearly 700 former teachers – indeed all 1,095 teachers who have been disbarred for whatever reason since 2015 – have retained whatever pension entitlements they previously accrued.

The power exists to strip teachers of their pension if they commit treason (thankfully still not the most common of occurrences), serious violations of the Official Secrets Act (there will be little in the way of classified information lying around most schools) or offences committed in connection with their role that could damage confidence in the profession or are “gravely injurious to the state”.

But this power has not been exercised in recent decades. No teacher has lost their pension since at least 2005. The power is also rarely used in relation to others in the public sector – under the NHS pension scheme just 33 people have been stripped of their entitlements since the 1970s.

Now, victims groups are calling for this to change and for those who have so despicably breached the trust placed in them to lose their pensions.

One must have sympathy with this line of argument.

Few sane people would wish to stand up for the rights of paedophiles. Those who abuse children in their care fully deserve to be punished to the full extent the law permits.

There is a very strong case for awarding the pensions of abusers to their victims. Such actions are not just criminal, they are also civil wrongs for which victims are entitled to sue for compensation and seize assets from their abusers.

But in terms of the state stripping people of their pension entitlements, let us be careful what we wish for.

This is a dangerous road to go down, especially in the hands of our current Labour Government and the woke educational and NHS blob. What starts with a clampdown on sex abusers could soon end up with public sector workers losing their pensions for wrong-speak. The legions of the debanked – almost half a million customers last year – could then be joined by the unpensioned.

This is not an argument about the sustainability of public sector pensions and whether the current arrangements can be justified. The Teachers’ Pension Scheme costs taxpayers around £9bn per year. The total liability of public sector pensions is estimated at £5.8tn.

If all these schemes were closed now, annual costs would still rise for decades – peaking at £132bn in 2061.

The whole system is in urgent need of reform, but that is a completely different issue from individuals losing their entitlements for misconduct. In addition, that public sector pensions are such a costly behemoth is not the fault of individual teachers, nurses or doctors. It is a consequence of decades of cowardice by politicians of all parties, finding it easier to avoid having to take difficult decisions.

The reason why unpensioning miscreants is generally wrong, I would argue, is that don’t imagine if ministers start to get a taste for such action they will stop with such offenders. Again and again, policies which have started as a means of combatting a genuine issue have morphed into a much wider bureaucratic power grab.

Just take a look at the Prevent programme at schools, set up to combat Islamist extremism. Examining the scheme now, you might imagine that the biggest domestic threat to British national security did not come from Islamic State adherents and their fellow travellers, but from the extreme Right.

In the year to the end of March 2024, 45pc of referrals were for “extreme Right concerns”, almost double the 23pc referred for “Islamist concerns”. Another 18pc were referred for the rather catch all-sounding “conflicted ideology”.

The case against unpensioning people we wholly disapprove of is the same as why we should champion free speech for those with views we abhor.

Standing up for the rights of people we broadly agree with to express their views is simple. The real test is whether we are willing to stand up for the rights of those whose views we hate.

It is the easiest thing in the world for someone of my world view to be outraged if a university bans a speaker for their Brexit-y, gender critical or pro-Israel views. It is much more difficult – and for that reason all the more important – to stand up for freedom of expression for trans activists and those taking, say, a virulent anti-Israel stance.

If we clamour for the unpensioning of those who are genuinely reprehensible, we will have handed another tool to the Whitehall machine to regulate behaviour they don’t approve of. And who knows who will be next in their sights.

By past performance, it could well be those expressing views which run counter to the prevailing consensus. And those views will generally be of a conservative persuasion.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Michel Roux Jr kitchen designer goes under in blow to middle classes
data.description:
A kitchen designer that developed ranges with celebrity chef Michel Roux Jr has collapsed into administration in the latest sign of a crisis for middle-class homes.

Moores Furniture Group has this week appointed administrators in a move that places hundreds of jobs at risk, with 124 employees being made redundant immediately.

The company has brought in advisors from Interpath to handle the administration process. The 75-year-old company had previously been controlled by turnaround firm Hilco before a management buyout last September.

Interpath said 336 employees had been retained for the immediate future to “work through certain work in progress”, including processing existing orders for customers.

However, there is no certainty about positions for those employees after the work is completed.

Its collapse is the latest sign that companies catering to the middle classes are struggling. Tile maker Fired Earth similarly collapsed late last year, while kitchen brands Smeg and Aga have both also recently warned over weaker demand from cash-strapped customers.

Moores is one of Britain’s largest privately owned kitchen manufacturers, supplying housebuilders including Barratt Homes as well as developing ranges sold in Homebase.

In 2020, it struck a deal to develop kitchens with Michelin-starred chef Mr Roux Jr, described as a “luxurious range”.

At the time of the partnership, Mr Roux Jr said the kitchens were based on “principles from a chef’s workflow”. 

He added: “When I’m cooking, the things that very much make a difference for me are great lighting, clever storage and a sensible layout. 

“So these were the things I wanted to make sure we got absolutely right to create a professional kitchen for the home.”

The Roux Kitchens range started from £4,470, although some designs were priced much higher. The range appears to have since been discontinued, with the latest social media posts in 2021.

On Monday, Wren Kitchens, a rival kitchen designer, said it had been in talks to save Moores but had been unable to reach an agreement.

However, Wren has agreed to deliver Moores’ outstanding customer contracts “with absolutely no impact on price, delivery schedules or administration costs”.

Wren said it would also “see if there are opportunities to provide alternative employment to a significant number of Moores sales, operations and internal support teams”.

Wren has also bought certain intellectual property of Moores as part of the pre-pack deal.

The turmoil at Moores Furniture comes amid a growing crisis among middle-class furniture and homeware brands.

Last autumn, Italian fridge maker Smeg said Britain’s “challenging economic landscape” had dampened demand from customers.

The parent company of Aga, which makes expensive ovens seen as a status symbol in country homes, said it had been “negatively impacted” by inflation after reporting a sharp drop in UK sales.

‘Challenging trading conditions’

In November, Fired Earth, which specialises in handmade tiles for kitchens, bathrooms and floors, was forced to shut all of its high street stores after entering administration. Topps Tiles later bought the brand out of administration.

Moores Furniture Group’s latest accounts – filed for 2024 – show sales were down 8.3pc over the year, while losses after tax rose from £7.3m to £8.7m.

The company said sales had taken a hit from fewer houses being built by private developers. The results had also included a £2m blow from the collapse of Homebase, where Moores had sold furniture.

Interpath said Moores “had experienced challenging trading conditions in recent months, exacerbated by rising input costs and low levels of housebuilding activity”.

James Clark, managing director at Interpath and joint administrator, said: “The strong headwinds facing the UK construction industry continue to have an impact on companies up and down the supply chain.”

data.title: Why Elon Musk’s threat to buy Ryanair may be more than a joke
data.description:
Elon Musk has always had outsized ambitions, from colonising Mars to implanting chips in people’s brains. Now the world’s richest man has embarked on arguably his most audacious challenge yet: winning an argument with Ryanair chief Michael O’Leary.

On Monday, Mr Musk made the outlandish suggestion that he could buy Ryanair, Europe’s largest airline, as part of an escalating row with Mr O’Leary over an in-flight Wi-Fi service.

Both men have locked horns in recent days in a bubbling feud pitting two of the world’s most outspoken figures. Both have branded the other “idiots”.

On Monday, the billionaire upped the ante by asking his 230 million followers on his social media platform X a yes-or-no question: “Buy Ryan Air and restore Ryan as their rightful ruler”.

Within hours, hundreds of thousands of people had responded, with 80pc saying “yes” and backing Musk’s attempt to launch a bid for the airline.

While it might sound like idle banter, the billionaire has a track record of following through on seemingly outlandish plans. After all, the first sign that Mr Musk would buy Twitter came when Mr Musk wrote: “I love Twitter.” A user responded: “You should buy it then,” to which Mr Musk replied: “How much is it?”

The suggestion that Mr Musk could buy Ryanair follows a falling out between the billionaire and Mr O’Leary over the airline’s decision not to use SpaceX’s Starlink satellite Wi-Fi on its planes.

The spat started after the airline said it would not use Mr Musk’s internet technology on any of its 640 aircraft because the required antennas would increase drag and raise fuel costs. Mr O’Leary said the service would cost it up to $250m (£186m) a year.

Mr Musk called Mr O’Leary “misinformed” and argued that Ryanair had miscalculated the fuel impact of Starlink’s technology. “He got the fuel impact calculation wrong by a factor of 10 and refuses to look either at physics-based calculations or measured fuel usage for 737s that fly Starlink. Fire this imbecile,” Mr Musk wrote last week.

However, the Ryanair boss doubled down on his position during an interview with Newstalk, an Irish radio station, on Friday.

“What Elon Musk knows about flights and drag would be zero,” he said. “We’ve already had Boeing confirm we have to put an aerial antenna on top of the aircraft; now, that will cost us about a 2pc fuel drag.

“Our fuel bill a year is five billion; it would cost us about $200, $250 million a year. In other words, about an extra dollar for every passenger we fly, and the reality for us is we can’t afford those costs.”

Mr O’Leary added: “I would pay no attention whatsoever to Elon Musk. He’s an idiot – very wealthy, but he’s still an idiot.”

Meanwhile, the official Ryanair account – known for salty responses to passengers – also poked fun at Mr Musk. When X briefly went offline last week, the company posted: “Perhaps you need Wi-Fi @elonmusk,” and later called Wi-Fi on planes “propaganda”.

Mr Musk responded: “How much would it cost to buy you?” In a second post, he said: “I really want to put a Ryan in charge of Ryan Air. It is your destiny.”

On Monday, he wrote: “Michael O’Leary is a retarded t--- who needs to be fired. Put him out to pasture! Please make sure he sees this message.”

While it may seem like little more than bravado, Mr Musk has a track record of doing what he says he will do – however unbelievable it might seem.

Mr Musk launched The Boring Company after getting frustrated at traffic in Los Angeles, writing: “Traffic is driving me nuts. Am going to build a tunnel boring machine and just start digging ...” He later added: “It shall be called ‘The Boring Company.’”

He has also launched a series of Tesla products purportedly as jokes, including a flamethrower, short shorts and tequila, while he has named features of the car, such as its Ludicrous mode, after the 1987 comedy spoof Spaceballs.

A long-running fondness for the Doge meme, which features a Shiba Inu dog and the garish comic sans font, also led to a series of doge-themed ventures, such as Tesla accepting the meme cryptocurrency Dogecoin and Donald Trump’s department of government efficiency (Doge).

Mr Musk took over what was then Twitter in a $44bn deal in 2022, after months of speculation about a bid for the platform largely centred around the billionaire’s missives on the platform. He has since renamed it X.

Prediction markets, which bet on the likelihood of events, suggest there is at least a chance of a deal. The two most popular sites, Kalshi and Polymarket, put the chances of a deal at 19pc and 7pc respectively.

Ryanair’s share price rose by 1.2pc in opening trading on Tuesday before falling back.

Mr Musk is the world’s wealthiest man with an estimated net worth of $681bn, meaning a deal for Ryanair would be well within his means even at its €30bn market value.

Mr O’Leary has been at the helm of Ryanair since 1994 and is one of Ireland’s most successful businessmen.

His airline has been ranked the “worst short-haul airline” by consumer champion Which?, facing criticism for seat comfort, boarding experience, food, cabin environment and cleanliness, as well as “mediocre” value for money.

However, Mr O’Leary has transformed it into one of Europe’s most successful airlines thanks to his obsessive focus on cost.

Mr Musk’s Starlink runs a constellation of 9,500 low-Earth-orbit satellites that deliver internet connections from space. The service aims to provide coverage to homes, even in remote rural areas, while also serving as a potential solution to patchy railway signals. The company says its internet service has about nine million customers.

Airlines have become an increasingly important customer base. Over 30 carriers, including British Airways, Lufthansa and United Airlines, have announced deals to use its internet service.

Ryanair did not respond to The Telegraph’s request for comment.

data.title: Britain risks missing out on gas price ‘lifeline’
data.description:
Britain risks missing out on an economic boost from collapsing gas prices as green levies keep energy costs high, a new report has warned.

A global glut of natural gas will see wholesale prices fall sharply across the UK and Europe, potentially almost halving by the end of the decade, according to leading energy consultancy Wood Mackenzie.

Analysts said falling prices offered “a lifeline” to UK and European heavy industry and “could provide material support”.

However, Britain and Europe risk missing out on the benefit because surging carbon taxes will keep energy costs high. The two main taxes on burning gas for power currently add about £25 per megawatt hour (MWh) to costs in the UK, and the levy is designed to ratchet upwards, potentially doubling by 2030.

Massimo Di Odoardo, vice president of gas research at Wood Mackenzie, said: “UK and European prices are set to almost halve compared to levels in 2025.

“However, lower wholesale gas prices will not fully translate into lower energy bills for industrial users across the EU and UK. Taxes and levies and the need to pay higher prices for carbon emissions will limit some of the reduction.”

The prediction will add to scrutiny of Labour’s net zero plan, which involves racing to eliminate gas from the energy system by 2030. Critics say the artificial deadline is creating high costs for homes and businesses.

Labour is also preparing to link Britain’s carbon taxes to the EU’s, a move that has already led to a jump in costs for industry.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has argued that transitioning the UK to green energy will boost energy security and lower bills, but industry leaders warn that even if that happens, it will be too late.

Sir Jim Ratcliffe, chairman of the chemicals conglomerate Ineos, has warned that carbon taxes on oil and gas have left the UK unable to compete with rivals. Ineos shut down its Grangemouth Refinery last year and has warned that its neighbouring chemical works may follow.

The cost of gas and the associated carbon taxes have become a crucial issue for heavy industry. The output of industrial firms such as paper mills, steelmakers, glassblowers and pottery fell by a third between 2021 and 2024 due to high energy prices, according to a recent analysis by the Office for National Statistics.

Wood Mackenzie’s report warned: “The single most important obstacle to increasing European industrial competitiveness going forward is its ambitious decarbonisation agenda.

“Even as natural gas prices fall, industries could face higher overall energy bills.”

Wood Mackenzie’s gas price predictions are based on expectations that global supplies of liquefied natural gas (LNG) will grow faster than demand.

“LNG suppliers, particularly in the US and Qatar, have responded to high gas prices by investing massively in new supply,” its analysts wrote. “Consequently, traded gas prices in western Europe are set to tumble in the coming years as a new wave of LNG supply hits the market.”

Andreas Schröder, head of gas analytics at energy specialists ICIS, backed Wood Mackenzie’s findings. He said recent investment was “likely to result in periods of oversupply, which is broadly positive for European industry as it puts downward pressure on gas prices”.

American gas prices are currently far lower than Europe’s, but are expected to rise because export terminals being built along the US East Coast to export LNG will effectively turn the USA and Europe into a single market, equalising prices. It means US gas prices, which are now less than half those in the UK, are likely to surge 40pc by 2030.

A Government spokesman said its aim remained to cut the UK’s reliance on imported gas.

They said: “We are getting off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets controlled by petrostates and dictators, and with our record-breaking renewables auction round, we are taking back control of our own energy. The new renewables we are procuring are 40pc cheaper than new building and operating new gas – and will drive down wholesale prices.”

data.title: Drug-driving causes more road deaths than alcohol
data.description:
Drugs are now a bigger factor in road deaths than alcohol, official figures show.

The number of deceased drivers who tested positive for drugs increased by 78 per cent – from 106 to 189 – in the decade to 2023, according to the Department for Transport (DfT) and police data.

By contrast, the number of dead motorists with alcohol proven to be present in their system rose by five per cent in the same period, from 162 to 171.

Sir Keir Starmer now stands accused of ignoring the bigger problem of drug-driving while planning to reduce the drink-driving limit. The Government’s proposals have prompted a backlash from MPs and publicans, who say they will put pubs under more pressure following an increase in business rates.

Britain lost an average of one pub each day in 2025, and industry bosses have warned that rising tax bills and wages, on top of higher energy costs, will drive hundreds more out of business.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save the UK’s pubs, calling on Labour to stop its assault on locals and cut tax and red tape.

Ministers are now expected to reverse their decision to impose higher costs on Britain’s struggling pubs, saying they are working on relief measures to be announced in the coming days. But the about-turn relates to jumps in business rates for landlords, not the new drink-drive limit.

DfT figures show that the percentage of fatal collisions in which drink-driving was involved has been relatively stable over the past 10 years, at 13 per cent.

However, the proportion in which drug-driving played a role doubled from five per cent in 2014 to 10 per cent in 2023.

While drug-driving convictions rose by 13.5 per cent to 27,000 in 2024, the number of drivers convicted of drink-driving offences fell by six per cent to 36,415.

Meanwhile, injuries from drink-driving incidents have significantly decreased since 1980, from around 20,000 annually to about 5,000 since 2020.

Despite this, the Government’s new road strategy proposes “taking tougher action on drink-driving” by reducing the legal limit of 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg, or around a pint.

It would be the most significant reform to road safety laws since 1967, when the blood alcohol limit was first introduced.

Chris Philp, the shadow home secretary, said: “Labour are now proposing even more measures that will endanger country pubs.

“At the same time, the Government is completely failing to do more to address a more rapidly growing road safety issue – drug-driving. More drivers killed in a collision had drugs in their system than alcohol.

“The Government should prioritise toughening up on drug-drivers above measures which will strangle struggling country pubs.”

The British Beer and Pub Association warned that any toughening of measures on drink-driving would harm rural pubs in areas without public transport or reliable taxi services.

A spokesman said: “The pub sector continues to face huge challenges, so any additional policy measures that further impact trade will be of real concern to licensees, especially those in rural areas.”

‘Geographical lottery’

Drug-drivers face similar penalties as those caught drinking, including a minimum 12-month driving ban and up to six months in prison for serious or repeat offences.

Limits for illegal drugs such as cannabis, cocaine, ketamine and heroin are set at extremely low levels, but not at zero in order to account for accidental exposure.

However, Government-funded research has suggested that dangerous drug-drivers have been escaping prosecution – and putting lives at risk – because some police forces ration the number of testing kits issued to officers to just one a day.

The study, by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, found there was a “geographical lottery” where the best-performing forces were catching 10 times more drug-drivers per head of population than the worst.

Drivers can also escape justice because of delays of four to five months in processing blood tests. Officers have only six months to prosecute.

Penalties to be reviewed

The Government’s new road safety strategy proposes that there should be a review of penalties and mandatory training for drink and drug-driving offences.

It has also pledged to explore alternative processing and evidence collection for drug-driving to “improve speed of results, supporting more robust enforcement outcomes.”

A DfT spokesman said the strategy would “save thousands of lives by targeting the root causes of deaths, including the impact of both alcohol and drugs”.

They added: “We’re determined to crack down on drug-driving, and the strategy includes new measures to modernise how we tackle it, including new testing methods, and powers to suspend driving licenses for those caught under the influence.

“We do not expect the new limit to harm pubs – experience in Scotland shows such changes have minimal impact on local businesses while making roads safer.”

Government sources also denied that the strategy failed to focus on tackling drug-driving, pointing to plans for roadside tests to catch drug-drivers to be stepped up.

Motorists suspected of driving under the influence of drugs would also have their licences suspended immediately under the plans. Roadside tests to catch drug-drivers will also be stepped up.

Police can already use roadside tests as a preliminary check, but results must be confirmed by requiring the driver to take a blood test in a lab. The strategy will set out plans to allow the police to charge suspects based solely on a roadside test.

However, ministers accept that this power will only be used when more reliable tests, that can assess exact amounts of drugs in people’s blood, are available. They also want to encourage police to use roadside tests more widely.

data.title: Hotel bosses warn of price rises unless Reeves expands pub tax relief
data.description:
Rachel Reeves is under pressure to expand pub tax relief measures to hotels after the likes of Hilton warned holidaymakers faced price rises without urgent action.

More than 100 hotel bosses including executives at chains such as Hilton, Butlin’s and Holiday Inn have warned the Chancellor that soaring business rates will drive up costs for hard-pressed holidaymakers.

Ms Reeves is understood to be finalising a support package for pubs after weeks of campaigning by publicans, trade bodies and The Telegraph’s Save Our Pubs campaign against the high taxes and energy costs facing the industry.

However, the about-turn has dragged Labour into a row with the wider hospitality sector, which faces sharp tax rises from next April after a sudden jump in business rates.

In a letter to Ms Reeves, organised by trade body UKHospitality, the hoteliers said: “These changes to business rates present the most significant challenge to accommodation providers in terms of their ongoing viability, and many will face tough decisions in terms of employment and their ability to invest.

“This issue will grow in severity over the coming years as transitional relief tapers off – this is not something businesses will be able to easily absorb, while passing costs on to guests would further add to the cost of living pressures already greatly affecting the British public.”

UKHospitality estimates that the average hotel’s annual business rates bill will increase by 115pc – equivalent to an additional £205,200 in the next three years.

One executive at a leading UKHospitality group said: “Quite right that pubs are going to get some relief from this ridiculous tax increase, but what about the restaurant next door that, like the pub, serves food and drink?

“Why should they be penalised for not calling themselves a pub? The whole sector has been hit by increased National Insurance, increased utility costs, and increased rates. The Employment Rights Act will make life even harder for people trying to run a business and further put up the prices that consumers have to pay.”

Hospitality businesses were some of the hardest hit by Labour’s decision to raise employers’ National Insurance and the minimum wage in 2024.

Ms Reeves also removed pandemic-era tax relief for hospitality in her most recent Budget, to be replaced with what the Chancellor described as the “lowest tax rates since 1991”.

But following the Budget, many business owners discovered that their property values had separately been sharply uprated, triggering steep tax rises. Hundreds of publicans banned Labour MPs as part of an industry-wide lobbying effort to save the sector from further closures.

On Wednesday, Ms Reeves confirmed pubs would receive “more temporary support”. However, no detail has yet been announced.

Kate Nicholls, of UKHospitality, said: “Hotels and holiday parks are the hardest hit by business rates hikes, facing 115pc increases.

“As this coalition so clearly sets out, this will only have adverse impacts on employment, investment, and, in some cases, business viability.

“Accommodation businesses will, unfortunately, have no choice but to pass these additional costs on to the consumer, which will only worsen the cost of living crisis and drive inflation.

“The Chancellor has recognised that hospitality needs further business rates support – it’s crucial the entire sector receives that much-needed support.”

A government spokesman said: “We’re backing hospitality businesses with a £4.3bn support package to limit bill rises, alongside capping corporation tax at 25pc, cutting red tape and taking action on the cost of living to boost high streets.”

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.
