data.title: BP’s use of Chinese steel is a betrayal of British manufacturing
data.description:
Steelmaking is on its knees in this country, of that there can be no doubt.

British Steel has been under the control of a government-appointed receiver since last April, after ministers were forced to step in and save it from bankruptcy.

As the first anniversary of that bailout approaches, there is no word on when or how it will be returned to private ownership despite the bill to taxpayers rapidly approaching the £300m mark.

The same fate has befallen industrialist Sanjeev Gupta. Once nicknamed the “saviour of steel”, his Speciality Steels UK business collapsed into compulsory liquidation last summer, prompting a scramble in Whitehall to find a buyer.

Yet five months later, Britain’s third-biggest steel manufacturer remains in state hands.

In Port Talbot, things are even more grim.

Traditional steelmaking at the South Wales site vanished in 2024 after more than 100 years. Three thousand people lost their jobs and the effect on the surrounding communities is widely considered to have been devastating.

Armed with a £500m taxpayer-funded grant, its Indian owners Tata planned to turn it into a hub for so-called “green steel”.

With the future of such a vital sector never more bleak, a government announcement in the autumn that British-made steel should be prioritised in big UK manufacturing contracts was a highly welcome lifeline.

What a shame, then, that BP, a genuine titan of British industry, together with its partners on a multibillion-pound energy project in Teesside, apparently didn’t get the memo.

The decision to use Chinese steel to help build Net Zero Teesside – a £4bn green power plant – has rightly prompted outrage, as well as demands for answers from bewildered ministers.

Whichever way you cut it, there is no reasonable excuse for handing a Chinese steelmaker a multimillion-pound order when British Steel has said it is capable of providing the materials.

No wonder that Chris McDonald, the industry minister, has said he will be meeting BP “urgently” to discuss this.

It’s bad enough that this is happening on the site where the Redcar steelworks once stood before it too had to close its doors for good.

As Lord Houchen, the Tees Valley Mayor, rightly said, it is a “betrayal” of British workers, British manufacturing, and those who lost their jobs when the Redcar site had to be mothballed.

This is doubly so given one of the reasons for the disappearance of the Teesside plant was that it was unable to contend with a steep slump in steel prices triggered by a tsunami of state-subsidised Chinese imports.

But that’s not even the worst of it.

What makes this such an egregious snub is that Net Zero Teesside – or NZT as it is imaginatively known – is one of the largest taxpayer-funded projects being built today.

Set to be the world’s first gas-powered electricity plant with its own carbon capture and storage system, and capable of powering around one million homes, support for the project was unveiled to almost farcical levels of fanfare back in 2024.

Even Sir Keir Starmer joined in on the act. Here was proof that Labour was investing in our “industrial heartlands” after being “held back by 14 years of instability and low growth”, the Prime Minister proclaimed.

What’s more, this magical “home-grown energy” would also mean “more money in working people’s pockets”, while simultaneously protecting billpayers “so tyrants like Putin can’t attack the living standards of working people again.”

Phew. No wonder the Government is throwing money at this thing to make sure it gets off the ground.

Not only has the plant been the beneficiary of billions of pounds in government grants, finance, and loan guarantees to help bankroll its construction – the site will also receive massive state subsidies once it’s up and running.

Through a so-called Dispatchable Power Agreement, the Government guarantees the plant a minimum income for 15 years, even if electricity prices drop.

This cosy arrangement is similar to the Contracts for Difference regime through which Ed Miliband has thrown untold billions at wind power providers as part of his green crusade.

Yet despite being handed what some might consider equally lavish sums to bankroll the development of unproven carbon capture technology, BP and its fellow backers – Equinor and TotalEnergies – have awarded China’s Modern Modular Engineering and Construction Company a £5m contract to provide seven million tonnes of steel.

The move becomes even more infuriating when you consider that the same type of steel is available from domestic suppliers including British Steel, a company that British taxpayers have also been propping up – to the tune of £1m a day for the last nine and a half months.

It’s certainly not the sort of joined-up thinking that would form the basis of a coherent industrial strategy.

In the summer, there was much excitement after the Government unveiled plans for new public procurement rules that would require major infrastructure projects to use UK materials including steel.

This isn’t state overreach as some devoted free marketers might claim. It’s just plain common sense.

Yes, UK-made steel might be more costly than its Chinese equivalent, but any premium will surely be recovered from the ripple effect that comes from job creation and regional growth alone.

Why would you allow China, a country that has done more to undermine British industry than any other, to benefit at the expense of the UK economy? It’s pure self-harm.

But as BP has demonstrated, such stipulations count for little if they’re not compulsory.

Nor does it say much for all the excitable promises that have been made about how the millions of jobs destroyed through de-industrialisation will be replaced by even more green ones.

On the contrary, the folks up in Teesside claim that the reason a Chinese supplier was chosen over British Steel is because it comes from environmentally friendly electric arc furnaces, as opposed to the dirty blast furnaces up in Scunthorpe.

Once again, it is hard to escape the conclusion that the benefits of Labour’s clean power crusade have been wildly overstated.

As for BP, it should reverse its decision immediately and show that the “British” in British Petroleum still counts for something.

data.title: Uncovered: Secret room beneath Chinese embassy that poses threat to City
data.description:
China is to build a hidden chamber alongside Britain’s most sensitive communication cables as part of a network of 208 secret rooms beneath its new London “super-embassy”, The Telegraph can reveal.

This newspaper has uncovered detailed plans for an underground complex below the vast diplomatic site in central London, which Beijing has sought to keep from public scrutiny.

Despite the apparent security risk, Sir Keir Starmer’s Government approved the embassy on Tuesday morning. 

Later this month, the Prime Minister is due to visit China and meet Xi Jinping, the Chinese president.

The plans for the embassy, which are redacted in all publicly available versions, can only be revealed because The Telegraph has uncovered the unredacted documents.

The drawings show that a single concealed chamber will sit directly alongside fibre-optic cables transmitting financial data to the City of London, as well as email and messaging traffic for millions of internet users.

The same hidden room is fitted with hot-air extraction systems, possibly suggesting the installation of heat-generating equipment such as advanced computers used for espionage. The plans also show that China intends to demolish and rebuild the outer basement wall of the chamber, directly beside the fibre-optic cables.

The disclosures will reignite fears that Beijing could be seeking to spy on the information carried through the network, potentially gaining access to state and financial secrets.

Following the decision, MI5 and GCHQ admitted in a letter to Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, and Yvette Cooper, the Foreign Secretary, that it was “not realistic to expect to be able wholly to eliminate each and every potential risk” linked to the embassy.

Other areas obscured by redactions include emergency back-up generators, a sprinkler plant, new lift shafts and communications cabling. The plans also conceal bathrooms and showers, potentially allowing officials to remain underground for extended periods.

Covering 22,000 sq m on the site of the former Royal Mint, the embassy would be the largest Chinese diplomatic mission in Europe.

The project has been mired in controversy since Beijing first sought planning permission under the previous Conservative government, not least because of its proximity to sensitive communications infrastructure. Dominic Cummings, Boris Johnson’s former chief adviser, has claimed that MI5 and MI6 warned him China was “trying to build a spy centre underneath the embassy”.

Most of the planning documents submitted to Tower Hamlets council were heavily redacted for what China described as “security reasons”  a move that led Angela Rayner, then housing secretary, to delay approval and demand clarification.

The Telegraph can now reveal the unredacted plans, offering the clearest insight yet into China’s intentions for the hidden areas of its new diplomatic headquarters.

The secret room

Of the 208 rooms exposed by The Telegraph, the most striking lies beneath the Seamen’s Registry building on the north-west corner of the former Royal Mint site.

Its exterior wall borders Mansell Street, a busy artery at the Tower Hill interchange.

Plans show a triangular underground chamber measuring up to 40m across and 2-3m deep. Crucially, they indicate that the outer basement wall facing Mansell Street would be demolished and rebuilt.

It is not yet known which company China will appoint as its contractors to build its planned new embassy, or who will be responsible for demolishing and rebuilding the Seaman’s Registry basement wall.

But that construction work would place Chinese officials just over one metre from the fibre-optic cables running beneath the pavement – raising the prospect that they could be tapped.

Telecoms cable plans seen by The Telegraph show the fibre-optics here belong to companies including BT Openreach, Colt Technologies and US telecoms giant Verizon Business.

Carrying signals bearing the innermost financial secrets of the British economy, the cables stretch between the Telehouse group of data centres in Docklands and other centres around the capital. Linked together, these form the core of the London Internet Exchange (Linx). Beyond London, they connect to Atlantic cables linking to the US.

Linx is one of the biggest internet exchange points in the world, handling vast volumes of data spanning everything from financial transactions to instant messages and emails.

Its cables carry the financial transaction data relied upon by banks to update withdrawals and deposits, such as ordinary people’s salary packets and payments for goods bought online.

Further drawings from the plans of the secret room suggest at least two air-extraction systems will be installed, venting through an existing lightwell and a new grille – implying the need to remove large volumes of hot air from the basement.

One explanation is that China plans to install extensive computer infrastructure as part of an espionage operation linked to the Mansell Street cables.

Professor Alan Woodward, a security expert at the University of Surrey, said: “China won’t say what the basement is for. It could be legitimate classified communications equipment – but that can hide a multitude of sins.”

“If they wanted to tap the cables,” he added, “they wouldn’t need to go far. You wouldn’t know what was happening down there.”

Options for spying on the data could include diverting cables, inserting a wire tap or even placing a device directly on top of the cables. The fibres could even be bent so that light leaks through their casing, allowing it to be read by specialised equipment.

Prof Woodward described the demolition of the basement wall as a “red flag”.

“There’s a long history of cable-tapping by East and West alike. Anyone who can do it has done it.

“Espionage isn’t just about state secrets. Economic intelligence is central to the mission of foreign intelligence services.

“If I were in their shoes, having those cables on my doorstep would be an enormous temptation.”

China may, of course, have benign reasons for installing cooling systems. The space could house a data centre serving embassy staff, or even a gym or cafeteria.

Even so, the proximity to Britain’s most critical communications infrastructure will deepen concern.

The site borders the Wapping Telephone Exchange on three sides. The BT-owned exchange serves an area of roughly 2 sq km, encompassing hundreds of City firms, including Lloyd’s of London, as well as landmarks such as the Gherkin and the Walkie-Talkie.

Nearby cables also connect 11 data centres forming part of the London Internet Exchange.

Any nation state would be tempted to snoop on such traffic – particularly one with which Britain’s relations have been so fraught.

Flats and bathrooms

Plans also show proposals for flats within the Embassy House building on the eastern side of the site.

Detailed layouts suggest luxury accommodation for senior diplomats, alongside smaller studio-style units. There is no obvious evidence of the detention facilities feared by some critics.

Basement drawings reveal further living infrastructure, including toilets and showers at the southern end of Embassy House. Adjacent rooms vary in size, with the largest suitable for a gym. Others, smaller and more secure, lie behind winding corridors and multiple doors.

The plans also show lift shafts, power and communications ducts, and a tunnel linking the Seamen’s Registry to a vast car park beneath the complex.

Utility drawings indicate rooms for high-voltage electricity, water supply and the embassy’s official telecoms connections.

At the northern end is a large space apparently designated for emergency generators, confirmed by a ground-floor drawing marked “New emergency generator ventilation”.

A Government spokesman said: “National security is our first duty and government security experts have been involved throughout the process so far.

“As the Home Office and Foreign Office have previously set out, all the security implications of the planning application have been identified and addressed.

“An independent planning decision will be made by the Secretary of State for Housing Communities and Local Government in due course.”.

China’s London embassy did not respond to requests for comment.

BT subsidiary Openreach said it had “robust security measures in place” and worked with the Government to protect its assets.

Explore the unredacted plans in full

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: Hollywood billionaire’s battle royale over Warner Bros comes to Britain
data.description:
The billionaire boss of Paramount has held talks with the Culture Secretary to lobby against Netflix’s $83bn (£62bn) takeover of Warner Bros.

David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry Ellison, travelled to London on Thursday for a meeting with Lisa Nandy as part of a wider charm offensive designed to defeat Netflix in the escalating takeover battle.

Paramount is trying to convince shareholders to back its $30-per-share takeover offer and encourage regulators to obstruct Netflix’s rival bid, which was accepted by Warner Bros late last year.

Warner Bros has repeatedly rebuffed Paramount’s overtures, insisting that the $83bn deal agreed with Netflix last month remains superior.

Paramount’s campaign is centred on arguments that a merger between Netflix and Warner Bros would pose significant competition concerns owing to the combined company’s dominance in the streaming market.

Warner Bros owns Discovery+, which is home to TNT Sports, and HBO Max, a major US streaming service that is scheduled to launch in the UK in March.

Executives are also emphasising the impact of the deal on cinemas amid concerns that Netflix could reduce the number of films distributed for theatrical release.

In addition to the meeting with Ms Nandy, Paramount has met with officials at the Competition and Markets Authority and regulator Ofcom.

Senior Paramount figures also met with cinema executives who are fearful that Netflix will cut Warner Bros’ theatrical investments.

A source close to Paramount said the meetings were intended to build relationships after the Ellison family took control of the US studio in an $8bn deal last year.

Paramount owns Channel 5, while it has also made a number of major films in the UK, including the latest instalment in the Mission: Impossible franchise.

The planned takeover of Warner Bros will mark a major reshaping of Hollywood as traditional studios seek to build scale to compete in the cut-throat streaming era.

Any deal will also have significant implications in the UK, where Warner Bros employs around 4,000 people. The company has made a raft of blockbusters in its Leavesden studios near Watford in recent years, including Barbie and Wonka.

Paramount’s PR push

Netflix has played down competition concerns and insisted it is committed to releasing films in cinemas, though it has acknowledged that the regulatory review process could take up to 18 months.

Paramount would itself face regulatory scrutiny over its plans to merge two of Hollywood’s major studios, though insiders said they were confident the proposed deal would clear any reviews.

Mr Ellison and Paramount executives’ meetings form part of a broader charm offensive across Europe as the company seeks to shore up support from regulators in Brussels. Senior executives also recently met Emmanuel Macron, the French president, Bloomberg reported.

A source close to Paramount insisted Ms Nandy was the first port of call for Mr Ellison’s UK discussions, but did not rule out the possibility of a future meeting with Sir Keir Starmer.

The studio has tapped City PR firm Brunswick to help in its discussions, while US law firms Cravath, Swaine & Moore and Latham & Watkins are advising on the deal.

Paramount’s lead financial advisers are Centerview Partners and bankers at RedBird Capital, the US private equity firm led by Gerry Cardinale, which is also the company’s second-largest shareholder.

Mr Cardinale, who sits on Paramount’s board, is also thought to be working with Greenlight Group, a regulatory advisory launched late last year by Ali Nikpay, Daniel Gieve and Adam Atashzai.

Mr Gieve is a former chief executive of the Government’s Office for Investment.

Mr Atashzai also briefly held an advisory role in No10 under the Conservatives, while Mr Nikpay is a former partner at law firm Gibson Dunn.

Paramount ramped up its campaign this week by suing Warner Bros in an effort to secure “basic information” that it said had not been shared with investors.

It has also outlined plans to appoint directors to the Warner Bros board and fight a proxy battle against the Netflix deal.

However, the company was dealt a setback on Thursday when a Delaware judge rejected a request to fast-track its lawsuit. Paramount has given Warner Bros shareholders until January 21 to accept its bid, though this deadline could be extended.

Paramount and the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport declined to comment.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Thousands of rail workers to lose jobs under Labour’s nationalisation plans
data.description:
Thousands of rail workers are to be made redundant as part of Labour’s plan to nationalise the railways.

Network Rail is making cuts to its workforce as it faces a budget shortfall potentially as high as £1.8bn, insiders said.

An estimated 2,000 employees out of the organisation’s total headcount of 41,000 could be laid off in the coming months.

Network Rail bosses told staff the proposed job cuts were needed as part of preparations for the track maintenance company to be absorbed into Great British Railways (GBR).

Earlier, Rachel Reeves revealed Labour could spend up to £45bn of taxpayers’ money on the Northern Powerhouse Rail project, including a possible extension of the controversial HS2 railway line to Manchester.

An all-staff message issued by Clive Berrington, Network Rail’s group director of route services, suggested the cuts were necessary to cope with “increased demands” and in preparation for the company’s merger with GBR.

He said: “We are facing increased demands that require additional resources, particularly in areas such as cyber security, new telecoms services, and lifts and escalators.

“We are also evaluating what needs to be done to get Route Services ready for GBR.”

Dave Hooper, Network Rail’s managing director for its southern region, said in a separate bulletin that there was a £350m gap in funding for his area alone between now and the year 2029.

The bulletin, seen by The Telegraph, said: “We’ve explained the demands on our funding and that there’s no additional money available.

“In fact, our [funding] settlement assumes we deliver significant efficiencies, which increase every year of the Control Period.”

Cuts to affect junior managerial grades

GBR is at the heart of Labour’s flagship plan to take passenger trains into full state ownership and control, ending rail privatisation reforms introduced by John Major in the mid-1990s.

The redundancies will affect staff in Bands 1 to 4, which are understood to be junior managerial grades. Affected staff will be formally placed at risk of redundancy from next Tuesday, The Telegraph understands.

Network Rail’s taxpayer funding is allocated in five-year control periods. The current period, known as CP7, ends in 2029, which is the latest year in which a general election could be held.

Speaking to the BBC on Tuesday morning, Ms Reeves claimed Labour’s promised increase in public spending on the railways in the 2030s is “actually going to happen”.

She said: “We’re doing this properly, doing this right. Because I want to give confidence to people that this is actually going to happen.

“It’s going to improve your train links, whether you live in Pudsey or Rotherham, or Newcastle or Darlington, or in the centres of Manchester and Liverpool, and Bradford and Sheffield.

“You’re going to see benefits from this.”

Although Labour has said the total budget for Northern Powerhouse Rail is capped at £45bn, just £1.1bn of taxpayers’ money has been allocated to design and feasibility studies so far.

Michael Dnes, a transport consultant, suggested this week that Labour’s announcement about using the former HS2 route between Manchester and Birmingham for a new, standard-speed railway line had only come about because legal powers authorising land purchase and track construction are due to expire soon.

“In case you’re unclear, yes, this is HS2 coming back – at least in the sense that it’s the HS2 rail line,” he said, adding that the line is “not being built at any measurable point in the future”.

A rail industry source who requested to be anonymous said: “It’s all linked to the creation of GBR and resizing the organisation for that.”

Regarding the redundancies at Network Rail, a spokesperson said: “We’re committed to delivering a safe, reliable railway while reducing costs for taxpayers and meeting our £3.9bn efficiency target for this control period.

“To achieve this, we’ve reviewed how we operate across the business, including management structures, to ensure we’re as efficient as possible.

“Our review is now concluding, and we’ll be sharing proposals with colleagues and trade unions for discussion over the coming weeks before any final decisions are made.”

The RMT trade union confirmed it was aware of the redundancy plan but said it would not affect the trade union’s members, who are frontline workers rather than managerial staff.

Network Rail later denied claims that its redundancies were linked to Labour’s rail nationalisation plans. 

A spokesman said: “GBR has not yet been created. The work ongoing at Network Rail is about delivering our efficiency commitments for CP7 – it is not part of the planning for GBR, nor linked to nationalisation or Government decisions.”

The Transport Salaried Staffs’ Association, which represents affected managers, did not respond to a request for comment.

The DfT declined to comment.

data.title: ‘I have 48 tins of tomatoes’: The middle-class obsession with bulk buying
data.description:
Stepping into Costco for the very first time, one could be forgiven for thinking the end of the world is nigh. On my maiden outing last month, other shoppers looked like they were stocking their doomsday bunkers, heaving trolleys loaded with pallets of mineral water, slabs of meat the size of a small child and enough toilet roll to supply an army regiment.

In truth, I arrived at Costco’s vast Southampton outpost already sceptical. Bulk buying, I had always thought, belonged to a species of consumer that wasn’t me: hyper-organised, storage-rich and willing to put inordinate effort into saving just a couple of pounds. I had also assumed a narrow range of joyless products – jeroboams of cheap ketchup, sack-loads of indifferent flour – and because I don’t have the space to stockpile my groceries, I was fully prepared to buy nothing at all. I just wanted to see what all the fuss was about.

At first, the cavernous warehouse space and industrial shelving stacked with gigantic tins, jars and sacks, arranged in no apparent logical order, did nothing to convert me. It felt less like grocery shopping – something I generally enjoy – and more like navigating a storage facility that happened to sell food. But Costco isn’t a global behemoth for nothing, and my epiphany duly came in aisle 12. An immense jar of dried wild mushrooms – the kind of specialist goody I didn’t expect to find – was extraordinary value: at £10.99 for 340g, almost 70 per cent cheaper, gram for gram, than the stingy packets I regularly buy at Waitrose.

Once I had my eye in, it was all systems go. A chunky 1.4kg container of chia seeds (I eat them for breakfast daily, woe is me) for £7.99 was, again, roughly two-thirds cheaper than at Waitrose. Then there was a hunk of 30-month-aged parmesan, a little over £20 for 1.48kg (more than 50 per cent cheaper), and a litre of Iliada extra virgin olive oil – a brand favoured by chefs – for £7.99 (£11-13 elsewhere). With my two adult children and husband toing and froing to taste samples and forage, our trolley filled with unseemly speed. But really, I shouldn’t have been surprised.

Costco UK is an outpost of a vast American membership warehouse club founded in Seattle in 1983, with more than 900 warehouses globally and 145 million members. As I learnt, it sells almost everything one would expect from a mainstream supermarket, but in bigger quantities: fresh fruit and vegetables, meat, fish, cheese and baked goods, alongside a vast range of non-perishable food and household items. The curveball aisles are an added draw: a widescreen television, contact lenses and a leaf blower can be yours in a single sweep.

UK branches are thriving

In the US, Costco is an institution, ranked among the largest companies in the Fortune 500, its annual membership model binding shoppers in for the long haul. Strict membership criteria notwithstanding – only business owners and certain professions qualify, with fees ranging from £30 to £84 depending on the plan – the UK arm is thriving. Last year it reported pre-tax profits of £188.9m, a 35 per cent rise on the previous year. Twenty-nine Costco warehouses are now scattered across England, Scotland and Wales, with at least 14 more reportedly on the horizon, including one under way in Gloucester.

That financial success and growth speaks to a shift in the way many middle-class Britons are shopping. My fellow trolley-pushers weren’t stockpiling for Armageddon; they were chasing value and convenience. It’s a trend that took hold during the pandemic, when shortages – most memorably of toilet roll – pushed people into shopping big for key items, says Nick Carroll, the director of retail insights at consumer group Mintel. And it didn’t stop when restrictions were lifted. A Barclaycard survey in 2022 found that 35 per cent of Britons admitted to stockpiling everyday items such as pasta, tinned food and toilet roll, as rising prices replaced lockdown as the dominant concern. “Consumers have held on to those more savvy shopping behaviours,” Carroll says. “Bulk buying is certainly more popular than it was five or six years ago.”

Perhaps surprisingly, the well-off are leading the charge. “It’s more common among middle-income and affluent households,” Carroll explains. “If you’re on a lower income, you’re unlikely to buy six bottles of something you won’t need for many months, because that money is better spent elsewhere. Typically, it’s people who are being electively savvy, or simply cautious, and who have the money to spend a little more now in order to make savings further down the line.”

A walk-in pantry helps

Mintel research underlines this pattern. In 2023, 17 per cent of British consumers shopped with Costco, rising to 30 per cent among households with incomes above £75,000 – the same group most likely to have the space, whether a larger home, spacious garage or walk-in pantry, to store the bounty from these mega-shops.

Costco isn’t the only way of buying big. Many do it closer to home, through a proliferation of multibuy offers or larger pack sizes at mainstream supermarkets. Online shopping has made this easier still. Mintel reports that more than a quarter of Britons who shop online do so specifically to bulk buy, as home delivery avoids the back-breaking problem of transporting heavy loads home.

Beyond the supermarkets, a small but established group of UK-based online wholesalers – including Bulk Buy Direct, Low Price Foods and Approved Food – is selling large formats of discounted food, often surplus or sometimes near or past their best-before dates. Food subscriptions are also on the rise, with small, quality brands in particular offering significant discounts for multiple items delivered on a regular schedule.

Bold Beans, a specialist in premium beans sold in jars, says subscription revenues in 2025 rose by 60 per cent on the year before. “Customers tell us that subscribing makes meal planning easier,” a spokesman says, adding that fans of the product simply don’t want to run out. On the subject of running out, one friend, who prefers the Mutti brand of tinned tomatoes, on hearing a rumour that a poor harvest could cause shortages in coming months, recently hurried to “the big Tesco” to buy 48 cans. These now occupy most of her larder space.

Coffee subscriptions are percolating too, driven by soaring and volatile bean prices. Rave Coffee, an independent Gloucestershire roastery, says it saw a 56 per cent increase in subscriptions last year compared with 2024. “Customers want the certainty they won’t run out of coffee and won’t blow the budget,” says David Webb, its marketing director.

Kev Lewis, the founder of Coffee Blog, says coffee lovers are increasingly looking for predictability, both in price and quality. “Subscriptions allow customers effectively to lock in a price, which feels reassuring in an uncertain market, almost like buying a small future in a commodity.”

An evolutionary drive to save

But bulk buying delivers benefits beyond the financial, say enthusiasts: it’s simply reassuring to know that favourite products won’t run out. This taps into something essentially human, says consumer psychologist Philip Graves. “It’s almost an instinctive response to want to stock up, which connects to a primary evolutionary drive to save,” he says. “Historically, humans did better once they realised there would be times when resources were scarce, and that storing helped protect them.” It’s also appealing to reduce the number of shopping trips for dull basics, leaving more time for more enjoyable things, Graves adds.

For all these reasons, podcaster Olly Mann, 44, from Hertfordshire, is a Costco evangelist. He’s been shopping there since childhood – his father was an early convert – and his nearest branch is a convenient 20-minute drive away. “I buy in big quantities for the convenience of not having to think about certain things again and again,” he says. “For really boring items like dishwasher tabs and loo roll, you buy big packets and you literally don’t have to think about them again for months.”

Mann doesn’t price-compare before he shops, and acknowledges that some items, such as meat, aren’t necessarily cheaper than at his local supermarket. But he knows his way to the bargains. Kirkland Signature is Costco’s private-label brand, which Mann says is high quality. “Their filter coffee [1.4kg for £18.99] lasts me months of daily home brewing,” he says. Now and then he treats himself to a vast bottle of Kirkland French vodka (1.75 litres for £40.99). “It keeps me in martinis for a year,” he says. Wrapping paper, cards and boxes of chocolates are also regular Christmas purchases. “The quality is what you might expect at Harrods, but for a fraction of the price.”

Catherine Balavage Yardley, a freelance writer from Wandsworth, bulk buys everything from toothpaste to children’s clothes. A working mother of four, including a newborn, and without a car, she shops big online from Amazon, Ocado, Boots, M&S, Tesco and Sainsbury’s. Saving money, she says, isn’t the main driver.

She is partly motivated by a fear of running out of essentials. “During Covid, our garage was probably half full of water and other stuff,” she says. But stocking up is mainly about making life easier. “It saves me so much time, which is more valuable to me than anything else.” She rarely buys just one of anything, opting instead for multiple or oversized packs of soap, toothpaste, toilet roll, children’s clothes and shoes, toys (for birthday presents), fruit, bottled water and milk. And bourbon biscuits. “My kids go through tons and tons of those,” she says.

Convenience, though, has its costs. Although her home is large, storage is still an issue when shopping on this scale; cupboards are crammed and the garage absorbs the overspill. “If you opened it up you would just see chaos and a million Amazon boxes,” she says. As a result, she sometimes forgets what she has bought and ends up buying duplicates. “Sometimes you can overbuy,” she admits. On one occasion, children’s shoes she had squirrelled away surfaced too late. “By the time I remembered they were there, he’d grown out of them,” she says.

Al Baker, a money-saving expert and author of the Penny Pincher blog, says storage is only one of the challenges when buying in bulk. A devotee of shopping big – “much to my wife’s displeasure” – he argues that it only really works if you are disciplined. Without that, he says, it’s easy to overspend on things you don’t need or simply don’t have room for.

He also points out that bulk buying is only really an option if you have the money to spare. “If you’ve got a few quid sat in the bank and you think you can afford to do it, brilliant. If you haven’t, that’s an issue.”

Baker’s home sounds like a small warehouse as he explains where he stores his monthly big shop. A chest freezer takes pride of place in the living room, filled with portioned meals he preps and freezes every Sunday, while he has equipped a cupboard under the stairs with shelves to hold toilet roll and kitchen paper. That cupboard now acts as a brake on his shopping habits: if there’s no room left, he won’t restock, no matter how good the bargain – though he admits the rule doesn’t always hold, and the back of his van is currently filled with overspill.

More important than storage, Baker says, is doing the sums properly before a bulk shop, rather than assuming a bumper pack is automatically good value. That means checking unit prices, comparing like with like across retailers, and factoring in other deals. “Supermarkets can be surprisingly competitive once you take account of multibuy offers, loyalty card discounts and cashback,” he says. “You have to factor all of that in.” He recommends tools such as Trolley, a free UK grocery price-comparison site and app that lets shoppers compare prices across major supermarkets and see where a basket comes out cheapest.

Another hazard is over-indulging. You might steadily plough through a vast supply of dishwasher tabs, but put a case of chocolate bars within easy reach and it may disappear far faster than it ever would in smaller quantities.

‘Meat can be more expensive’

Sophie Bowd, from Swindon, is well aware of these pitfalls. She and her partner spend £900-1,000 a month on a main Costco shop for groceries and household items for themselves and their four children, topping up elsewhere during the week. “We used to shop at Asda or Tesco, but we were spending about £1,500 a month,” she says. “Now we do one shop at Costco, then a weekly top-up of about £50.” That amounts to a saving of around £300-400 a month.

Bowd, who works in finance and runs a TikTok account charting her shops, knows that buying in bulk doesn’t necessarily mean securing a bargain. “The meat can be more expensive, especially beef, but it’s always better quality, so I don’t mind paying more for that,” she says. Snacks for children’s lunch boxes deliver some of the biggest savings, as does milk. “You can get two big cartons for under £3, almost double what you get in a normal shop for the same price.”

But shopping like this involves a full day’s work. Bowd’s nearest Costco, in Reading, is a 45-minute drive and, with four children – one still in a pram – the family can only manage one trolley at a time. It’s a slog, she admits, but one that pays off. “I’ve always got food in, so we never fall into the trap of getting takeaways,” she says. “And I’m saving money.”

If the idea of bulk-buying your entire shop gives you conniptions, a selective approach might make more sense, particularly for items where prices are expected to keep rising this year. Coffee is a good place to start, with global supplies under pressure. Lock into a subscription or buy big bags and freeze small batches in airtight containers or freezer bags.

Olive oil is another candidate, with prices expected to remain high and unpredictable. Buy large tins or bottles – the oil you use every day is better for this than the special stuff – store somewhere cool and dark, and decant into smaller containers as you go. Chocolate, too, looks unlikely to get cheaper. Dark chocolate can keep for up to two years if stored somewhere cool, dry and dark in an airtight container. Milk chocolate should store well for a year. Beef prices could also rise this year, so if you eat it often and have the freezer space, buying in bulk and freezing portions could deliver savings.

Bulk buying only really works when it’s based on what you already consume, argues Graves. “Think back to what you’ve used consistently over the last few months,” he says. “Our past behaviour is generally our best guide to what you’ll use in the future.” In other words, the safest bulk buys are often the boring ones.

He’s not wrong. I’m still staring at a vast (1.5kg) jar of pretzels I bought at Costco – something I never usually buy but convinced myself I’d get through over Christmas. It’s still two-thirds full.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Britain at risk of electricity rationing before general election
data.description:
Britain risks electricity rationing by the next general election unless its fleet of ageing gas-fired power stations is urgently upgraded, a new report has warned.

More than a dozen of Britain’s gas-fired power stations are so old that they may not last until the end of the decade and key parts required to upgrade them take years to arrive on order, energy consultancy Watt-Logic said.

It called on Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, to face up to the threat amid growing concern over UK power supplies, which still rely largely on gas despite the expansion of renewables.

“Without urgent action to secure dispatchable generation and stabilise the gas network, the UK faces escalating risks of supply shortfalls and widespread system failures well before 2030,” the report warns.

“It will be difficult to meet existing demand without rationing, let alone any additional demand from electrification.”

About a third of the UK’s electricity is generated by gas-fired power plants – but on windless days, this proportion can double.

Kathryn Porter, from Watt-Logic, warned that in five years time – just after the next general election – many of those gas plants could be offline and there are currently no plans to replace them.

She said a third of the fleet had been built in the 1990s and “while most received upgrades in the 2000s, in most cases this did not involve replacing rotors [a key component prone to wear]”.

“This means the anticipated retirement dates are becoming imminent and there is no replacement and minimal upgrade plans in place,” she added.

It means the risk of electricity rationing during the winter months will increase over the next five years – peaking between 2028 and 2031 – because of the loss in energy production.

The loss will be made worse by the planned shutdown of the UK’s ageing nuclear stations. Eight of the 10 remaining reactors face closure before or in 2030.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “This report lays out in expert detail how Britain’s net zero plans are taking us down a path of economic ruin. If ministers were truly interested in making Britain rich again, they would ditch their green ideology and take her report on board.”

Ms Porter warned that Scotland faces a particular peril because it has just two power stations capable of keeping power flowing through its grid – and both are at risk of failure.

Other gas-fired plants at risk of failure include Medway, Saltend and Seabank, all operated by energy giant SSE.

Connah’s Quay and Cottam, run by Uniper, and Little Barford, run by RWE, are also under threat.

Some new power stations are in planning or construction, including some gas-fired plants – but not enough to replace likely losses.

The report follows similar warnings from organisations including the National Energy System Operator (Neso), which last November warned that Britain faces the risk of severe gas shortages.

Responding to the report, a Neso spokesman said: “Great Britain has one of the most secure energy systems in the world... our engineers are confident Britain’s grid will continue to operate safely and securely as more renewables connect to the network in the years ahead.”

A spokesperson for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “The National Energy System Operator,  has been clear the faster we decarbonise, the more secure we are. 

“Gas will continue to play a key role in our energy system as we transition to clean, more secure, homegrown energy. 

“That’s why we are also delivering the biggest upgrade to Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to deliver clean power by 2030 and beyond.”

Uniper said it was confident its power stations would keep operating.

SSE, RWE and the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero were approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Treasury doesn’t understand risks of shadow banking bubble, Lords warn
data.description:
Treasury ministers have come under fire from an influential group of peers for failing to grasp the risks of Wall Street’s shadow banking bubble.

On Thursday, the House of Lords’ financial services regulation committee heavily criticised the Government for having “demonstrated a limited grasp” of the risks posed to the financial system from the private markets boom.

The report said the Treasury had an attitude of “passivity”, which was all the more surprising given the potential risks to the UK’s financial stability from shadow banking and private markets.

The rapid growth of private credit, which is corporate loans made by non-bank lenders, has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

Concerns about weakening lending standards were fuelled last year by the collapse of two US companies – First Brands, a car parts supplier, and Tricolor, a subprime auto lender.

Jamie Dimon, the head of JPMorgan, warned that more “cockroaches” would emerge because of lax lending standards over the years.

Lord Hollick, a member of the committee and former boss of media giant UBM, said: “The Treasury itself needs to be curious about this and needs to be actively engaged in looking for this and finding this information and giving us their view because obviously a lot of savers’ money, pension funds and insurance companies are going to be tied up with this.

“Heaven forbid that anything should go wrong, but clearly it’s of sufficient size and importance for the Treasury also to take a careful look at it.”

While the Lords committee praised the work of the Bank of England, led by Andrew Bailey, it warned that the Treasury appeared to defer to the Bank, with limited engagement with the issue itself.

The report concluded that there was not enough information to conclude whether private markets were “systemic” – in other words, whether they could pose a risk to the wider financial system.

In particular, it praised the Bank for its plan to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock.

The exercise will run over two rounds, with results expected to be published in early 2027.

While tougher regulations for banks following the financial crisis have boosted their resilience, they have also pushed some of their traditional lending activities outside the more regulated banking sector.

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn (£138bn) and is the second-largest after the United States, the report said.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We have worked together with the regulators to significantly increase our focus of non-banks sectors in recent years and have a robust, flexible framework to protect financial stability.

“We are considering the committee’s recommendations and will respond in due course.”
